data.title: Cut But Not Broken: 3M's Rebound Is Just Getting Started
data.description:
Introduction

Historically speaking, the worst-performing stocks on the market are dividend cutters, including companies eliminating their dividend. This group of stocks turned a $100 investment in 1973 into $73. During this period, dividend growers turned this $100 bill into more than $14,000!

However, while the odds are against dividend cutters, they are not all bad!

One of them is the 3M Company (NYSE:MMM), which cut its quarterly dividend from $1.51 to $0.70 on May 14, turning the former Dividend King into a dividend cutter.

The good news is that 3M is one of the "good guys."

I started covering the company on September 29, 2023, again. Back then, I called it "A 30% Undervalued Dumpster Fire." Since then, shares have returned 75%, more than twice the return of the S&P 500 (SP500).

My most recent article on the stock was written on July 2, titled "3M: A Dividend Cutter With A Strong Buy Rating." The company has returned 32% since then.

With that said, I cannot blame investors who have sold the stock recently, as it took the company roughly six years before it bottomed and regained upside momentum. This has resulted in a ten-year total return of 71%, a mile below the market's 259% return.

Hence, the point of this article is to update my bullish thesis on 3M, as the company continues to prove it's one of the few cutters capable of returning to long-term dividend growth.

To make my case, I'm using its just-released earnings and other major developments that hint at more elevated capital gains.

So, as we have a lot to discuss, let's get right to it!

The Recovery Continues

3M is not an easy company to assess. This has at least three reasons:

  • It is a company recovering from contracting earnings and lawsuits.
  • This year, it spun off its healthcare segment.
  • It is still a cyclical company operating in an environment of contracting manufacturing expectations (see below).

The company also has a new CFO, Anurag Maheshwari, who used to be the CFO of Otis Worldwide Corporation (OTIS). Mr. Maheshwari's first earnings call was off to a good start, as he reported 18% year-over-year non-GAAP earnings-per-share growth, supported by 1% organic revenue growth. When adjusted for geographic prioritization and product portfolio initiatives, organic growth rose by 2%.

Moreover, operating margins improved by 140 basis points to 23.0%, allowing the company to generate $1.5 billion in free cash flow, 141% of its net income.

However, in light of my cyclical headwind comments, the company saw mixed results, as it encountered volatile industrial demand, strength in electronics, lower automotive build rates, and softness in consumer retail.

  • Safety and Industrial organic growth: 0.9%. This segment saw growth in areas like roofing and electronics.
  • Transportation and Electronics organic growth: 2.0%. Here, electronic sales offset a global decline in car production.
  • Consumer business organic growth: -0.7%. Rising home improvement sales were unable to offset the general pressure on consumer sentiment in other areas.

Most of my more regular readers will know that all of this is in line with the macroeconomic discussions we had in prior comments.

Despite a challenging market for cyclical companies, 3M's ability to achieve positive organic growth by focusing on secular trends and improving margins through productivity gains and restructuring is a great sign.

The only thing better than strong earnings is updated guidance.

A Path To Sustained Elevated Growth

As we can see below, the company raised the lower end of its full-year earnings guidance by $0.20, now targeting an EPS range of $7.20 to $7.30.

On top of good 3Q24 results, the company is confident in its strategic goals for 2024. These goals are based on three pillars:

  • Driving sustained organic growth: This includes investing in innovation and improving commercial execution.
  • Improving its operational performance: The company is focused on efficiency improvements across the board. This is key to accelerating margin improvements.
  • Capital Deployment: Disciplined capital management is crucial to improve shareholder value. This includes winning back the trust of investors who jumped ship recently. Given how stubborn most investors are (and rightfully so!), this could take a long time.

As these strategic priorities sound extremely vague and generic (every company focuses on sustained organic growth, improving, margins, and deploying capital effectively, etc.), let me add some color.

For example, to accelerate growth, the company is focusing on R&D, including the introduction of more new products. This includes reducing administrative burdens on engineers and speeding up SKU setup time from 100 days to 60. In other words, products are getting to market faster and engineers can be more creative.

It is also shifting its focus, as it moved 100 people within R&D (and added 50 more researchers) to new product developments, especially in automotive, aerospace, electronics, and semiconductors.

According to the company, this realignment aims to reverse a decade-long decline in new product introductions, with a 10% increase in new launches expected this year.

Additionally, it is improving the way it reaches customers and deals with suppliers. After all, having better products is only helpful if customers are aware and able to get their products on time.

In addition to training new staff and improving its sales force and distribution network, it has done an impressive job in solving critical supply issues that have led to business losses in the past. I added emphasis to the quote below.

[...] we've seen continued progress in delivering on-time in full or OTIF to our customers. I know we've lost business and have paid fines due to poor delivery performance, and I'm encouraged by the steady improvement we're making, ending Q3 at 89% OTIF, up 5 points since the beginning of the year and 10 points above Q4 of 2022. - MMM 3Q24 Earnings Call.

The company also increased its supplier performance. For many years, the company has dealt with subdued on-time supplier delivery rates in the low 60% range. Thanks to measures like better demand prediction, reliability is now in the low 70% range.

While investors wait for 3M to further improve their business, they enjoy well-covered dividends and buybacks. Year-to-date, the company has generated $3.5 billion in free cash flow. That's after $1.7 billion in capital expenditures and R&D and 2% above its net income. This is a sign of high-quality earnings, as more than all of it is turned into distributable cash.

It also allowed the company to return $2.7 billion to shareholders. $1.1 billion of this was indirectly returned in the form of buybacks. The good news is that after the cut, MMM still yields 2.1% with a payout ratio of just 39%. That's based on the midpoint of its new 2024 guidance.

Going forward, I expect dividend growth to return.

MMM Stock Valuation

Using the FactSet data in the chart below, 3M is expected to grow its EPS by 8% and 6% in 2025 and 2026, respectively. These growth rates are favorable in light of macroeconomic challenges. If we were to see a cyclical growth bottom, I expect the actual numbers to be substantially higher.

That said, working with current numbers and its long-term average P/E ratio of 18.2x, we get a fair stock price target of $152, 13% above the current price.

This is slightly above the $149 target I gave in my prior article.

As a result, I remain bullish on 3M.

While the company is not turning into a fast-growing industrial juggernaut anytime soon, I believe it has what it takes to turn into a sustainable dividend grower again. This time, it will have a streamlined business model, better innovation, improved supply chains, and a valuable life lesson from the 2018-2023 downturn.

Personally, however, I am not buying 3M because of my elevated industrial exposure through railroads, machinery companies, and aerospace players. Currently, there isn't room for more (overlapping) exposure.

Takeaway

3M's journey from a struggling dividend cutter to a potential dividend grower is a lesson in resilience.

Despite significant setbacks, including legal challenges and a dividend cut, the company has made impressive improvements to its operations and capital efficiency.

Moreover, its better focus on innovation, supply chain improvements, and disciplined capital management is driving a slow but steady recovery.

While 3M isn't out of the woods yet, I believe its earnings growth potential and improved fundamentals make it a strong contender for long-term investors.

Hence, I am very confident that 3M will regain its place as a reliable dividend grower in the years ahead.

Pros & Cons

Pros:

  • Strong (And Important!) Recovery: 3M is bouncing back with impressive earnings growth and improving margins.
  • Innovation Focus: The company is accelerating product development, especially in high-growth sectors like automotive and electronics.
  • Free Cash Flow & Dividends: With strong free cash flow, 3M continues to reward shareholders through dividends and buybacks, even after the cut. However, it could take time until it wins back the trust of dividend investors again (see my cons).
  • Valuation Upside: At current levels, 3M remains undervalued, with a cyclical growth bottom being an additional wild card.

Cons:

  • Dividend Cutter Reputation: The dividend cut still hurts many, making it tough to regain investor trust.
  • Cyclical Exposure: 3M is exposed to economic cycles, which may slow its growth in tough markets.

data.title: Chevron Vs. XLE: The Energy Giant Is A Better Bet Than The ETF
data.description:
CVX and XLE: Previous thesis and oil price change

My last work on Chevron stock (NYSE:CVX) was published in early September 2024. That article was titled "Chevron Stock: Oversold And Undervalued With Prices Near 52-Week Low (Technical Analysis)." As you can already guess from the title, the focus of the article is on the technical indicators. In particular, I explained why the technical trading patterns show significant selling pressure judging by the price-volume information and its Relative Strength Index [RSI] but the business fundamentals remain sound.

Since that writing, there have been a few developments surrounding the stock that are worth updating. Some of these developments impact the entire energy sector as represented by the Energy Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:XLE), and some of the developments impact CVX more in particular. I will start with the leading factor in the former category: Oil price. Later in the article, I will address the factors that are more unique to CVX, such as the relocation of its headquarters and also its recent expansion. In the end, the takeaway is that we consider CVX an overall better package compared to the energy sector in terms of profitability, consistency, and growth potential. Yet CVX is for sale at a discounted price relative to XLE. As such, we recommend potential investors who are seeking exposure to the energy sector consider CVX first (which we rate as a Buy) before XLE (which we rate as a Hold).

With this, let me start with the oil price change. The chart below shows the performance of CVX and XLE in the past year (top panel) together with the Brent Crude Oil Spot Price (BCOSPNK). As seen, oil prices have been under large pressure recently and have declined by 21.38% in the past year alone. The compressed oil price in turn has pressured the performance of both XLE and CVX, but unevenly. The XLE fund has experienced a small total return of 3.18% in the past year, lagging the overall market by a large margin but leading CVX. As seen, Chevron has suffered a total loss of -5.66%. Such performance divergence is unwarranted in our view and has made CVX a far superior investment opportunity over XLE, as detailed next.

XLE ETF: Basis information

Before going into further details, a quick summary. XLE tracks a market cap weighted index of energy stocks (U.S. only) listed in the S&P 500. More specifically, according to the fund description:

XLE offers liquid exposure to a market-like basket of US energy firms, including companies in the oil, gas and consumable fuels, and energy equipment and services industries as identified by GICS. XLE pulls its stocks from the S&P 500 rather than the total market, so its portfolio mainly favors large caps. Holdings are weighted by market cap, subject to a capping methodology that ensures no single security exceeds 25% at each quarterly rebalance.

More specifically, the next table lists the top 10 holdings of XLE. As seen, Exxon Mobil (XOM) is the largest holding, representing 23.18% of the ETF's total assets. Chevron follows closely behind with a 15.41% stake. CVX represents one of the world’s largest integrated oil companies, with significant operation in most of the steps along the value chain in the industry. Its upstream operations include exploring for, producing, and transporting crude oil and natural gas and processing, liquefaction, etc. Its downstream operations include refining, marketing, and transportation of energy commodities and products.

XLE Vs. CVX: Why we prefer CVX

The table below presents a comparative analysis of CVX and XLE, focusing on valuation, profitability and dividend growth metrics. Note that all the inputs used in this table were gathered from Yahoo Finance, Seeking Alpha, and the fund’s homepage as of Oct. 21, 2024. To us, this comparison clearly shows XLE to be a "diworsification" for CVX. Overall, CVX offers a combination of strong profitability, a solid dividend growth track record, and better consistency, but yet is undervalued when compared to XLE.

Let me start with profitability. In terms of returns on equity, CVX's ROE is admittedly lower than XLE's as seen (by about 20%). However, I think ROE underestimates CVX’s return on its investments, and ROCE (return on capital employed) is a better measure here. As detailed in an earlier article:

For businesses like CVX, I consider the following items capital actually employed 1) Working capital, including payables, receivables, inventory, 2) Gross Property, Plant, and Equipment, and 3) Research and development expenses (a relatively minor expense for a business like CVX) are also amortized and considered part of its capital employed.

Under these considerations, CVX’s ROCE is currently around 23% based on my estimate of its 2024 FY financials, far better than the sector’s 16.5%. Note that for the sector, I use the ROE to approximate its ROCE. For a sizable group of diverse stocks, the differences between ROCE and ROE tend to average out and thus become numerically close.

In terms of valuation, based on the price-to-cash flow ratio, CVX is currently trading at a premium relative to XLE (by about 6% as seen). However, in terms of P/E and P/B ratios, CVX's multiple are lower than XLE. To wit, its P/E ratio is lower than XLE by about 3%, and the P/B ratio lower than XLE by about 19%, suggesting noticeable undervaluation.

Although given CVX’s dividend champion status and the significant role of dividend payouts in the energy sector, we think dividend yields offer more insights into their valuation comparison, as detailed next.

XLE and CVX: Dividend yield in focus

Even though both CVX and XLE have maintained a consistent dividend growth rate of 6.4% in the past five years, CVX's longer track record of dividend increases offers far better consistency. As a dividend champ, CVX has a significant advantage with 36 consecutive years of dividend growth compared to XLE.

More importantly, the current yield spread [YD] between CVX and XLE sends a strong signal about CVX’s attractive valuation. The concept of YD and my method for computing it has been provided in our earlier article. Here I will only quote the gist:

  • Dividends provide a backdoor to quickly estimate the owners’ earnings. Dividends are the most reliable financial information and the least open to interpretation. In investing, we always prefer a simpler method that relies on fewer and unambiguous data points rather than a more complicated method that depends on more ambiguous data points.
  • The dividend yield spread [YS] is based on a timeless intuition. No matter how times change, for two securities that pay regular dividends, the yield spread between them always measures their risk premium relative to each other. A large spread provides a higher margin of safety and vice versa.

Against this background, the next chart illustrates the historical YS between CVX and XLE in the past 10 years, between 2014 and 2024.

As seen, throughout the period, CVX's dividend yield has been above XLE, as reflected by the long-term average YS of 0.211% as seen. The key observations in my mind are twofold.

First, under current conditions, CVX is trading at a spread far above the historical average (0.939% vs 0.211%). This spread is not only far above the historical average but also near the thickest level in the past 10 years as seen, indicating one of the largest relative valuation discounts to the sector.

Second, you can also see that the YS has exhibited significant volatility in the past and this volatility is primarily caused by XLE, not CVX. Notably, as you can see from the chart above, during the pandemic, the YS dropped to a bottom of near -8%. This is largely due to the extreme price correction that XLE has suffered (more than 50% off the peak of its pre-pandemic level as you can see from the chart below), which in turn caused a large yield surge. In contrast, CVX suffered far less correction as seen. The fact that a single stock can show far better strength during a major recession than an ETF serves as another key indicator of its resilience.

Other risks and final thoughts

In terms of downside risks, CVX and XLE share many common risks. The primary common risks facing the oil and gas industry include fluctuations in oil prices, regulatory changes, and geopolitical risks. Oil prices are inherently volatile, influenced by geopolitical events, economic conditions, and supply-demand dynamics. Regulatory changes, such as environmental regulations or tax policies, can significantly impact the industry's profitability.

For CVX, there are some additional risks that can impact it in more specific ways. CVX has been expanding its footprint globally. It has recently executed agreements in Namibia, Brazil, Equatorial Guinea, and Angola to increase its global exploration acreage position. Such expansion provides growth opportunities but also heightens its exposure to geopolitical risks. Domestically, the company is in the process of a $53-billion acquisition of Hess. The Federal Trade Commission has recently completed an antitrust review of the acquisition. The acquisition and the subsequent synergy (assuming the deal will be completed) could take some time and face some uncertainties, especially considering that part of the deal is that CEO John Hess will not be appointed to the CVX board after the acquisition. Recently, the company has also announced a relocation of its headquarters from San Ramon, Calif., to Houston. I expect this relocation to enable better collaboration and engagement among CVX’s executives, employees, and key business partners in the long run. However, in the near term, this move might increase costs and temporarily reduce efficiency.

All told, I believe CVX is a far more attractive investment than the average energy sector represented by XLE for several reasons. In particular, I consider XLE to be a diworsification of CVX under current conditions. I always prefer a single and well-managed company (or a few of them) with a strong track profitability record and reasonable overvaluation over diversification across multiple companies. This article compares CVX to XLE in terms of their financial metrics, valuation, profitability, and consistency. To me, this comparison clearly demonstrates CVX to be a better-rounded investment package compared to XLE.

data.title: Kingsoft Corporation: Positive Short-Term And Long-Term Prospects (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
I raised my investment rating for Kingsoft Corporation Limited (OTCPK:KSFTF) [3888:HK] from a Hold to a Buy. The near-term outlook for the company's online games segment has been boosted by the introduction of a new mobile game known as JX3 Ultimate, while its office software business is well-positioned to take advantage of China's increased focus on domestic or local software. I have become bullish on KSFTF considering the company's favorable prospects.

The earlier May 29, 2024 update was focused on the evaluation of KSFTF's financial results for the first quarter of this year.

Kingsoft Corporation's shares can be traded on the Hong Kong equity market and the Over-The-Counter market. The company's shares listed on the Stock Exchange of Hong Kong are very liquid with a mean daily trading value of around $25 million for the past three months as per S&P Capital IQ data. On the other hand, Kingsoft Corporation's OTC shares have poor trading liquidity. US stockbrokers like Interactive Brokers offer trading services for Hong Kong-listed shares.

New Mobile Game Is Expected To Be Near-Term Growth Driver

KSFTF noted in the company's 2024 interim report released late last month that "the mobile version" of its PC game JX3 Online known as "JX3 Ultimate was launched successfully in June." This is a significant development that is anticipated to boost the performance of Kingsoft Corporation's online games business segment in the near term.

Canadian gaming website wtfast describes JX3 Online as "a 3D martial-arts themed MMORPG (Massively Multiplayer Online Role-Playing Game) that features ancient Chinese swordsman legends." KSFTF's JX3 Online PC game was first introduced to the market 15 years ago, having boasted a few million users on a daily basis in 2020. With my previous July 19, 2021 write-up, I emphasized that "there is a compelling need for Kingsoft Corporation to launch new games" to "reduce its reliance on" its "flagship PC game" JX Online III or JX3 Online.

Kingsoft Corporation's new JX3 Ultimate mobile game launch is the right move to leverage on the popularity of JX3 Online to seek out new gaming revenue sources and diversify its games portfolio.

The new JX3 Ultimate mobile game is likely to have performed well, taking into account recent disclosures.

The producer in charge of the JX3 Ultimate did an interview (translated using Google) with Chinese media platform Sohu in August this year, which revealed key statistics pertaining to KSFTF's new mobile game. The number of monthly active users for JX3 Ultimate had surpassed the 10 million mark in the first month following its introduction to the market. There are two positive surprises worth highlighting. One surprise is that approximately 60% of JX3 Ultimate's monthly active users were JX3 Online's dormant players, and the majority of the dormant players which returned to JX3 Ultimate haven't played a JX3 franchise game for the past seven years. The other surprise is that the average age of new players (who have never played any JX3 franchise game previously) for JX3 Ultimate was 22 years, or much lower than JX3 Online's 26 years average player age.

There are three key takeaways from JX3 Ultimate's metrics disclosed by the game's producer in the recent interview cited above. Firstly, the JX3 Ultimate mobile game is popular, considering the monthly active user metric exceeding 10 million. Secondly, the new mobile game has rekindled interest in the JX3 gaming franchise, with a meaningful number of dormant players coming back to play JX3 Ultimate. Thirdly, KSFTF has managed to attract younger new gamers with this new mobile game offering.

In its 2024 interim report, Kingsoft Corporation highlighted that JX3 Ultimate's "cross-platform data inheritance and interconnectivity with the flagship (PC game) JX3 Online" drove "a new record in daily active users across all platforms" for the JX3 gaming franchise in the second quarter. This sends a clear signal that JX3 Ultimate has had a positive impact on the company's online games business.

KSFTF had raised the full-year FY 2024 revenue growth guidance for its online games business segment from +10% to +20% according to an August 22, 2024 research report (not publicly available) titled "Q2 Review: Upward Revision In Games Guidance" issued by Soochow Securities. As a reference, Kingsoft Corporation's online games business registered a +10.4% YoY increase in segment revenue to RMB 2,198 million for 1H 2024, which accounted for 47.7% of its 2024 interim top line.

Kingsoft Corporation's short-term outlook is favorable. KSFTF's new JX3 Ultimate mobile game has been a success, and this makes it more likely that the company's online games segment will meet its +20% segment revenue expansion target for the current year.

Office Software Business Has Good Long-Term Growth Potential

Kingsoft Corporation's other key business segment, representing 52.3% of the company's 1H 2024 total revenue, is its office software business. The office software segment's office software branded as WPS Office is a potential substitute for the software programs offered by Microsoft (MSFT) Office.

A recent October 18, 2024 South China Morning Post news report drew attention to China President Xi Jinping's comments indicating that the country "must accelerate the realization of high-level technological self-reliance." China's stance on increasing its dependence on domestic technology solutions providers is a tailwind for Chinese company KSFTF's office software business.

As per Reuters' October 2023 news article, "China spent 1.4 trillion yuan replacing foreign hardware and software in 2022" which was +16% more than what the country spent on this similar endeavor in the previous year. Reuters subsequently published another report in April this year, highlighting that China's "state-owned enterprises were instructed in 2022 to replace office software systems with domestic products by 2027." It is apparent that China is determined to diversify away from foreign software products in the coming years, as per these recent media reports.

Chinese research firm CCID Consulting published a research report (translated using Google) titled "2022-2023 China's ICT Ecosystem And PC market" last year. There are approximately 30 million existing PCs used by the Chinese government and the ministries, with new yearly PC purchases of around 5 million units based on CCID Consulting's estimates outlined in its 2023 research report.

Kingsoft Corporation's WPS Office software is dominant in its home market, with a 90% share of the office software market in China. This means that KSFTF's office software business is a major beneficiary of the growing demand for domestic office software in the Chinese market.

The segment revenue for KSFTF's office software business rose by +17.1% to RMB 4,557 million in the most recent fiscal year or FY 2023. Kingsoft Corporation's annual office software revenue growth could potentially accelerate to the low-to-mid-twenties percentage range in the medium-to-long term, riding on policy tailwinds. Assuming that the Chinese government continues to replace old PCs or buy new PCs at a rate of 5 million units every year going forward as per CCID Consulting's estimates, this translates into incremental office software revenue of over RMB 1 billion for China's office software industry and KSFTF (90% market share). An annual individual subscription plan for Kingsoft Corporation's WPS Office costs as much as RMB 240, which I have used as a reference for the estimated corporate subscription plan pricing.

The growth prospects for KSFTF's office software segment are excellent. Kingsoft Corporation's office software business is a leading domestic player in the Chinese office software market, which is benefiting from the country's emphasis on self-dependence relating to technology.

Key Risks

There are two major risk factors concerning Kingsoft Corporation.

The first risk to consider is the popularity of KSFTF's new JX3 Ultimate mobile game. If Kingsoft Corporation fails to keep JX3 Ultimate's users engaged, this game might witness a significantly slower pace of user growth in the coming months.

The second risk to watch is a change in the Chinese government's policy or stance on technology adoption. Assuming that China decides to move slower with the transition to domestic or local software, KSFTF's office software business could possibly underperform in the future.

Bottom Line

I have a positive view of the short-term prospects for Kingsoft Corporation's online games business and the long-term outlook for its office software business. KSFTF's appealing valuations based on a comparison of its earnings multiple and earnings growth rate also support my Buy rating. The market currently values Kingsoft Corporation at 21 times consensus next twelve months' normalized P/E, while the company's FY 2023-2027 consensus EPS CAGR is +51%. This translates into a very enticing PEG (Price-to-Earnings Growth) ratio of around 0.41 times, as per S&P Capital IQ data.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The 10-Year Treasury Rate May Rise Above 6%
data.description:
The United States 10-Year Bond Yield (US10Y) is at a crossroads and may have much further to climb. Despite the Federal Reserve cutting rates, economic resilience is pushing interest rates higher. If history is any guide, the 10-year rate could surge to 6% or beyond, driven by a stronger-than-expected economy, persistent inflation, a steepening yield curve, and increased US spending.

Given the recent run of strong economic data, it may be surprising that the Fed cut the Fed Funds rate by 50 bps in September because of a cooling labor market and inflation. However, the September jobs report showed better-than-expected revisions for July and August and depicted unexpected economic strength. Meanwhile, the September CPI was hotter than expected.

Steeper Yield Curve

Due to the robust data and Fed rate cuts, the yield curve is steepening as the front of the curve declines, expecting more Fed rate cuts, while the back of the curve rises due to better-than-expected growth. If the Fed and the Fed Fund Futures are correct, and the neutral rate is around 3 to 3.5%, then the 10-year rate is probably too low based on historical norms.

The 10-year rate is still trading 62 bps below the Fed Funds rate. That spread will likely rise if the Fed continues on its rate-cutting path, as indicated and based on current Fed Fund futures.

Currently, the Fed Fund futures see the overnight rate bottoming around 3.4% by December 2025. This would imply that the economy's neutral rate is somewhere in that region.

Going back to the 1960s, the 10-year tends to peak between 275 and 385 bps above the Fed Funds rate. If the neutral rate is indeed somewhere around 3 to 3.5%, it would mean, at a minimum, the 10-year rate rises to a range of 5.75% to 7.35%

Typically, the yield curve peaks when the Fed Funds rate falls and moves away from the 10-year. Therefore, at the current rate of around 4.20%, the yield curve would peak at most at just 120 bps. But the risk is that because we are coming out of a period where rates have been suppressed for so long, it is possible that the 10-year Treasury hasn't found its natural rate. Still, the chances of the 10-year rate falling as the Fed cut is unlikely unless economic growth slows or inflation falls below 2%.

2% Inflation

Since 1960, the US 10-year has tended to trade about 1.95% above the inflation rate, using the median. This would suggest that the 10-year rate probably has a bit more to rise from the current spread of 1.8%. But more importantly, this gives us a sense that if inflation remains around the Fed's target of 2%, then the 10-year rate should find a floor around the 4% region, provided there is no recession or inflation doesn't fall below 2%.

So again, if the yield curve continues to normalize and looks like it has in the past, it tells us that the 10-year rate isn't likely to see too much downside. Therefore, the risk is that the back-of-the-curve continues to push higher as part of the normalization process.

Term-Premium

Another factor that is difficult to account for is the US debt and deficit spending and whether the term premium in the 10-year rises or falls. Investors may simply demand a higher yield if the debt continues to mount and the spending deficit widens. Today's term premium is only 12 bps, which is very low by historical standards. Again, it is hard to say where the term premium will be in the future, but the risk, based on the state of US spending, is that the term premium goes higher, pushing 10-year rates higher.

Impacts On ETFs

The 10-year rate is likely to be determined by the neutral rate, the term premium, and the health of the US economy. But, at least based on the present data, the 10-year rate has much more risk of upside than downside. This would be bearish for ETFs such as the iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (TLT) or the iShares 7-10 Year Treasury Bond ETF (IEF). These focus on the longer end of the curve and would see the price of the ETF decline as the rate rises.

The next few months will be important in determining the 10-year rate's direction, and given how difficult previous months have been to navigate, it will probably be tough to assess the current state of the economy.

All one can do is remain vigilant and open-minded.

data.title: Franklin Resources: A Victim Of Passive Money
data.description:
There never was a good war or a bad peace.” - Benjamin Franklin

Shares of investment management firm Franklin Resources (NYSE:BEN) are down by more than 60% over the past 10 years as the paradigm shift from actively managed to passively managed money continues. The firm has attempted to stem the tide by making significant acquisitions, including Legg Mason, Lexington Partners, and Putnam Investments over the past five years. With a safe dividend yielding more than 6%, a P/E on FY24E EPS of 8.7, and an ongoing probe for a (likely isolated) case of alleged cherry-picking, the recent insider buying merited a deeper dive. An analysis follows below.

Company Overview:

Franklin Resources, Inc. is a San Mateo, Calif.,-based investment manager that provides sponsored funds, institutional and high-net-worth account programs, and other investment vehicles primarily in the U.S. and Luxembourg. As of Aug. 31, 2024, the firm held assets under management [AUM] of $1.68 trillion, managed by 1,500-plus investment professionals in 25 countries. Known in the industry as Franklin Templeton after its 1992 buyout of Templeton, Galbraith & Hansberger, the firm was founded in 1947 and went public in 1971. Shares of BEN trade around $20.50 a share, equating to an approximate market cap of $10.75 billion.

The firm operates on a fiscal year (FY) ending Sept. 30. For the avoidance of doubt, the 12 months ending Sept. 30, 2024, is FY24.

Growth at What Cost?

Franklin has grown through acquisition over the past five years. However, through the lens of its share price, it has not been a success. The first meaningful deal was Baltimore investment and asset management shop Legg Mason in 2020, in which the firm acquired $797.4 billion of AUM for a cash consideration of $4.5 billion. That transaction made it the sixth-largest independent investment manager in the world. It was followed by the purchase of private equity firm Lexington Partners, whose $55 billion of assets AUM were onboarded for $1.75 billion in April 2022. Shortly thereafter, European credit and private debt manager BNY Alcentra Group and its $35 billion of AUM were acquired from Bank of New York Mellon for an upfront cash consideration of $594.1 million (and earnouts up to $350 million) in November 2022. The most recent sizeable transaction occurred at the onset of 2024 with the purchase of Putnam Investments, in which Franklin paid Great-West Lifeco cash of $321.7 million, 31.6 million shares of stock (~$940 million), and earnouts up to $375.0 million for its AUM of $142 billion.

These moves were more or less to stem the massive outflows in its funds in the latter half of the past decade. In FY14, Franklin held average AUM of $887.9 billion. In FY19, just prior to the announcement of the Legg Mason acquisition, its average AUM had fallen 22% to $697.0 million in an appreciating market.

The chief culprit for this dreadful performance was the paradigm shift from active to passive investment management. The dirty little secret concerning large pools of money that are "actively managed" is that they really aren’t. Obviously, portfolio managers have bullish and bearish opinions on many stocks and bonds. However, when their investment charters dictate that they invest tens of billions of dollars into the market, it's simply impractical to execute for multiple reasons, one of which is the impact on share price when a manager is entering or exiting a massive position. As a result, these actively managed funds are essentially index funds in disguise, overweighting some positions and underweighting others in the stocks that comprise the benchmark against which they are being measured. Managers bonuses’ are typically tied to whether they beat a particular benchmark. As a result, it would be challenging to find a large actively managed fund beat (or fall short of) its benchmark index by 500 basis points or more. This dynamic also creates the patina of "responsible asset management" as the managers’ results never deviate too far from their respective benchmarks.

That said, the public slowly awoke to this nuance during the 2010s when it realized that it was better off investing in passively managed funds with extremely low (0.015% to 0.15%) expense ratios like the S&P 500 exchange traded index funds run by Vanguard, State Street (STT), and BlackRock (BLK), vs. the 0.55% to 0.80% fees that most of Franklin’s equity funds charge. Seeing that the world was onto the fact that investment management of publicly traded stocks and bonds was a commoditized product, active managers were compelled to lower their fee structures in an attempt to stem the outflows to passive managers. With this undercurrent in mind, Franklin’s acquisitions can be seen as either an attempt to lower its cost structure as a percentage of AUM (e.g., Legg Mason) or raise its average asset management fee as a percentage of AUM (e.g., Lexington Partners) by onboarding investment vehicles that command higher management and performance fees – not as the firm’s stated long-term plan of bland diversification across asset classes, investment vehicles, and geography.

These moves have not helped Franklin’s stock price, which has been on a steady decline, currently down 65% since peaking at an all-time high of $59.43 a share in 2014, save for a brief intraday descent below $15 during the pandemic selloff in April 2020.

3QFY24 Financials

With that substantial headwind as a backdrop, Franklin reported its 3QFY24 financials on July 26, 2024, posting earnings of $0.60 per share (non-GAAP) on revenue of $2.12 billion versus $0.63 per share (non-GAAP) on revenue of $1.97 billion in 3QFY23, representing a decline of 5% on an increase of 8%, respectively. Sequentially, the metrics were up 7% and down 1% against $0.56 a share (non-GAAP) on revenue of $2.15 billion in 2QFY24. Long-term net outflows totaled $3.2 billion. Ending quarter AUM of $1.65 trillion was up 15% year-over-year due to the addition of Putnam and positive markets.

Investment performance further proved the above thesis on active management, with 53%, 49%, and 52% of its strategy composite AUM outperforming their respective one-year, three-year, and five-year benchmarks.

Great-West Inquiry

The market more or less yawned at those results. However, when it was reported on Aug. 21, 2024, that a probe had been opened by the U.S. Attorney’s Office for the Southern District of New York (and parallel ones by the SEC and DOJ) concerning the alleged allocation of profitable trades to favored accounts (a.k.a. cherry-picking) at the firm’s Western Asset Management unit, Franklin’s stock tumbled 13% to $19.78 on substantial volume of 24 million shares. Franklin said that the manager in question had been placed on leave and that it was cooperating with the investigations after initiating one of its own. The outcome of this inquiry will likely be isolated, but the reputational hit to an active management firm could be slightly more disproportionate.

Balance Sheet and Analyst Commentary:

At this point, the most appealing aspect of Franklin is its quarterly dividend, which has grown by one penny a share every year after jumping three cents to $0.26 in 2018. Its current $0.31 distribution equates to a 6.0% yield. The firm’s balance sheet reflects cash and investments of $5.6 billion against debt of $9.6 billion, providing it plenty of capital to repurchase 7.1 million shares of BEN at a cost of $172.0 million in the first three quarters of FY24, leaving 34.8 million shares available under its current authorization.

With the paradigm shift in the industry acting as a structural impediment, it isn’t surprising to see the Street extremely negative on Franklin’s prospects, featuring seven hold, four underperform, and two sell ratings with a median price objective in the low $20s. On average, they expect the firm to earn $2.41 a share (non-GAAP) on revenue of $6.45 billion in FY24, followed by $2.57 a share on revenue of $8.9 billion in FY25.

Charles Johnson, who was the CEO from 1957 until 2013, still believes in his kids – CEO Jenny Johnson and Executive Chairman Greg Johnson – purchasing 100,000 shares of BEN at an average price of $19.08 on Sept. 11, 2024, increasing his beneficial ownership interest to 18%. UBS recently named BEN as one of 30 stocks that should benefit from recent interest rate cuts.

Verdict:

There's plenty not to like about Franklin as far as growth investment is concerned. It’s a giant barge with little operating leverage. That said, it kicks off significant cash and trades at 8.65 times FY24E EPS with a safe dividend that's likely to rise another $0.04 per annum in a few months. Its business model is in secular decline, but that transition will take many years to play out, providing it with all the hallmarks of a dead money, steady dividend stock, whose return can be enhanced via covered call orders.

data.title: Twilio: Show Me Quarter Coming Up (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Twilio, Inc. (NYSE:TWLO) has already seen a big run into the quarterly report next week, but the communications company hasn't actually boosted organic growth rates. The customer engagement platform business will need a big quarterly beat to justify a rally from here. My investment thesis is now more Neutral on the stock due to valuation.

Organic Growth Problems

Twilio has turned away from a big growth story to focus on profits. While the company is reporting solid organic growth beyond the reported growth rates, Twilio still only grew at a 7% organic clip in the last 2 quarters.

The company only guided to 6% organic growth in Q3. The disappointing part of the at story is that organic growth rates continue to slip.

Twilio did guide to only 4% to 5% organic growth for Q2 '24 and ended up reporting 7% organic growth again. The company beat the June quarter sales targets by $20 million, so the opportunity clearly exists to take the 6% targeted organic growth rate and end up with 7% organic growth again, or even top the rate and return to accelerated growth.

While Twilio has promoted AI growth catalysts, the customer engagement platform hasn't seen signs this will occur. The company recently integrated with OpenAI's new Realtime API to the Twilio platform, allowing for developers to build conversational AI virtual agents. Considering Twilio already had integrations with OpenAI last year to power generative AI capabilities, these AI opportunities just aren't driving material growth.

At the end of the day, the new CEO appears much more focused on growing operating margins. Twilio posted Q2 margins of 16.2%, up 460 basis point YoY. In addition, the company is now aggressively repurchasing shares with the large cash balance versus investing in product development while promoting GAAP profits.

Twilio repurchased $2.2 billion worth of shares for the year by the Q2 earnings calls and forecasted spending another $800 million by year-end. The total share count had already dipped 10% to 164 million shares outstanding, and the additional repurchases should eliminate another 11+ million shares.

Cash Flow Machine

Twilio was once a highly unprofitable company due to low gross margins from networking costs and heavy investing to build out the platform. Fast-forward, and the company produced 18% free cash flow margins in Q2.

Twilio has produced nearly $800 million in FCF over the last year. The stock only has an $11 billion market cap, so Twilio only trades at 14x FCFs, though the company appears to only target $650 million in FCF for the year.

The company more than doubled the FCF margin in the last year, but Twilio might struggle to grow the margin going forward. The communications platform only generates gross margins in the 53% range, limiting the amount of margin available to drop to the bottom line with the need to spend aggressively on R&D and even with cutting sales and marketing.

While this is all great, the company focusing the market on GAAP profits isn't ideal. The stock trades at 18x 2025 adjusted EPS targets of $3.84 and any focus on limited GAAP profits in the future would actually make Twilio appear expensive.

On the Q2 '24 earnings call, CFO Aidan Viggiano made the following remark on GAAP profit goals:

GAAP loss from operations was $19 million, as we continue to make solid progress on our path towards GAAP profitability.

The company even recently issued $20 million in cash bonuses versus stock-based compensation, which isn't the tradeoff investors want to see. Twilio had excessive stock-based compensation at $148 million in Q2 alone, amounting to 14% of revenues.

What investors would like to see is a reduction in stock-based compensation, or SBC, without switching the costs to cash. Or at least, the employees working in management and R&D likely collecting the SBC need to start producing higher organic growth to warrant the large SBC.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Twilio has definitely entered a show me period to drive the stock higher after the rally from $50 to $70. The customer engagement platform needs higher growth to warrant a further stock rise, while the focus on boosting margins and GAAP profits isn't likely to attract new investors.

Twilio needs to offer up a growth catalyst on the upcoming Q3 earnings on October 30, or the stock is stuck around $70.

data.title: Structure Therapeutics: Potential To Have Best-In-Class GLP-1 Agonist For Obesity Patients
data.description:
Structure Therapeutics Inc. (NASDAQ:GPCR) was able to report positive results from its phase 2a study using its GLP-1R agonist GSBR-1290 for the treatment of patients with obesity in June 2024. This is an important outcome because it now leads to another milestone for investors to look forward to, which is the initiation of the 36-week phase 2b study using this drug to treat these patients in Q4 of 2024. The scope of this biotech though is its ability to achieve rapid weight loss in a short amount of time as I will be going over below. However, another item to note is that it was able to switch from its capsule formulation to a tablet formulation and achieve substantial once-daily dosing weight loss for these patients.

Beyond this, the company is not going to take the chance of only developing this drug alone to achieve weight loss for these patients, it is also going to combine this GLP-1R agonist with other drugs from its pipeline like an APJR agonist ANPA-0073, GIPR selective agonist and amylin receptor agonist. The promise of this company is its ability to use other drugs for other indications from what I can see. Consider that ANPA-0073 is not only being developed to treat patients with obesity, but may also be used in treating patients with idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis [IPF]. Speaking of which, there actually even is another shot on goal in being able to target this large multibillion-dollar market indication.

This is in regard to the development of LPA1R agonist LTSE-2578 for the treatment of patients with IPF as well. A phase 1 randomized study for this program was initiated in June 2024 and is ongoing. The best part of all is that it has enough cash to get through several milestones because it states that it is well-funded to operate its business through 2027. The reason is that back in June 2024, it enacted an offering bringing in a large amount of cash on hand. With the ability to advance GSBR-1290 into many combinations for obesity, plus the ability to move some drugs in the pipeline towards the other large market indication of IPF, I believe that investors could benefit from any potential gains made here.

GSBR-1290 For The Treatment Of Patients With Obesity

As I have stated above, Structure Therapeutics was able to report positive results from its phase 2a study using its GLP-1R agonist GSBR-1290 for the treatment of patients with obesity. The goal of this company is to provide an alternative to currently available injectable drugs of the very same class. However, by improving safety and even possibly efficacy as well. Before going over the phase 2a results released from the completed study using this drug to treat these patients, it is first important to go over what obesity is and what the possible market opportunity for it could be. Obesity is a type of disorder characterized by an excessive amount of body fat. Consider that these patients have a body mass index [BMI] of 30 or higher. It is a type of chronic disease that sometimes can be managed with a change in food habits plus exercise, but lately, these drugs are also added into this mix to help these patients. The thing is that obesity just needs to be treated somehow. Why is that? That's because if left untreated, then it could lead to a host of other disorders such as the following:

  • Type 2 Diabetes [T2D].
  • Gallstones [stones that get stuck in the bile ducts and/or liver].
  • Fatty liver disease [Such as MASH or metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis].
  • Diseased kidney.

One analyst believes that the market for obesity treatments could expand to $200 billion by 2031. This is a very large market opportunity, but the thing is that GLP-1R agonists are expected to take a huge chunk of it. New drugs such as GSBR-1290 that is highly selective small molecule may offer an edge over them, but this remains to be seen in further clinical testing. It may be able to do this because the company's technology platform developed this drug to selectively activate the G-protein signaling pathway.

In order to see if the company would be capable of being able to treat these patients with obesity, it ran the phase 2a study which recruited patients with obesity and type 2 diabetes [T2D]. This 12-week double-blind, randomized controlled study recruited a total of 64 healthy or overweight patients and were then randomized to one of the following dose treatment groups as follows:

  • 37 patients were given 120 mg of GSBR-1290 once daily.
  • 27 patients were given a placebo once daily.

The final data released was highly positive, especially when you consider that in a short amount of time [12 weeks] patients were able to lose a significant amount of weight. It was revealed that in patients who took 120 mg of GSBR-1290 once daily, they achieved a significant placebo-adjusted mean weight loss percentage of 6.2%. This average weight loss during this period of the drug compared to placebo was achieved in a statistically significant manner, with a p-value of p<0.0001. The thing is that the percentage of weight loss achieved is competitive compared to other GLP-1R agonists approved or in clinical development. Why on earth does this company have a shot at potentially becoming the best-in-class GLP-1R agonist as an obesity treatment? Besides that, after only 12 weeks of competitive weight loss percentage, the low amount of safety events means that this company can explore higher dosing of GSBR-1290 in the upcoming phase 2b study. Not only does it have the ability to move to higher dosing, with its start low and go slow titration strategy, but it may almost have the benefit of providing patients with an oral drug option. Why is this crucial? That's because currently approved drugs such as Zepbound from Eli Lilly and Company (LLY) have to be given once weekly as an injectable type. I believe that when possible, an oral drug alternative will always be more welcomed than that of an injectable.

Speaking of an oral drug, the company also added another portion of this phase 2a study. It added a capsule to tablet Pharmacokinetic [PK] study to see if moving to this kind of oral drug would end up being better. This was a good idea to test out and believe it or not, but it actually ended up working out. It was shown that the patients given the tablet formulation of GSBR-1290 were able to achieve a slightly higher placebo-adjusted mean weight loss of 6.9%, which also beat the placebo with a statistically significant p-value of p<0.0001. The only thing to note is that Adverse Event [AE] treatment discontinuations from the phase 2a portion of the study were 5%, but in the tablet PK study, it went up a bit to 11%. Still, there were no elevated liver enzymes found, thus the ability for the company to move to higher GSBR-1290 dosing in the upcoming phase 2b study for this program.

Moving From GLP-1R Agonism For Obesity Patients To Combination Therapies

The thing is that despite Structure Therapeutics having already reported data from the phase 2a study, using GSBR-1290 for the treatment of patients with obesity, there are still several catalysts for investors to look forward to. The first of which is the initiation of the 36-week phase 2b dose-range finding study using this very same GLP-1R agonist to target these obesity patients. Such a mid-stage trial is expected to begin in Q4 of 2024 and from there, data from it will be released in Q4 of 2025. Even better, if the primary endpoint of this 36-week phase 2b study is met with statistical significance, then it would put the initiation of a phase 3 study in 2026. The thing is that there are a host of other types of catalysts for investors to look forward to. For instance, the company is also in the process of developing other drugs in its pipeline to be used in combination with GSBR-1290 as follows:

  • Oral small molecule Amylin drug program.
  • Oral small molecule GIPR drug program.
  • ANPA-0073 oral small molecule APJR program.
  • Oral small molecule GCGR program.

The thing is that several of these candidates being developed are also expected to be advanced in the clinic. Should each program turn out to be good on its own, then the end-game goal is to combine them together with GLP-1R agonists to increase the amount of weight loss that can be achieved compared to a single drug alone. The most notable one to go over would be the development of the Amylin inhibitor program because a candidate is expected to be nominated in Q4 of 2024. Upon this achievement, would then mean the initiation of a phase 1 study targeting obesity patients using an Amylin agonist drug in the 2nd half of 2025.

Pipeline Much More Than Just Developing Drugs For Obesity Patients

The thing is that while there are going to be multiple shots on goal in being able to target patients with obesity, there might be a way to capitalize on another large multibillion-dollar market opportunity. This would be with respect to the development of LTSE-2578 as an oral LPA1R agonist drug being developed for the treatment of patients with idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis. Before going over the advancements made thus far with this program, plus any possible catalysts to come about from it, it is first important to understand what this disorder is and what the possible market opportunity for it could be. Pulmonary Fibrosis is a type of disorder characterized by the lungs becoming scarred and/or damaged. The reason is that there is some kind of damage that causes the walls in the lungs to become thick and stiff. In turn, this means that the lungs can't carry oxygen throughout the body as they normally would. There are several symptoms that these patients with pulmonary fibrosis experience as follows:

  • Extreme fatigue.
  • Unexpected weight loss.
  • Muscles that hurt.
  • Shortness of breath.
  • Dry coughing.

It is then important to note how the term "idiopathic" comes into play here. The term idiopathic, in essence, means a disease arising with no known cause. Thus, a patient who has idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis has scarring/damage to the lungs with no known root cause. In essence, it just comes about randomly. The global idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis market is expected to reach $11.7 billion by 2031. This is another large market opportunity.

This company hopes to be in a position to target these IPF patients with an LPA1R agonist drug known as LTSE-2578. This particular drug is specifically being developed to target these patients. A phase 1 study for this program was initiated in June 2024 and then clinical data from it is expected to be released in the 2nd half of 2025. This program is highly important because it is believed that Lysophosphatidic Acid Receptor 1 [LPA1] receptor agonism might be implicated in removing excess collagen with respect to lung fibrosis injury. Having said that, or to take it one step further, Bristol Myers Squibb Company (BMY) has not only established a mechanism of action [MOA] in using LPA1R agonism against IPF, but it is in the process of running two phase 3 studies. This clinical candidate goes by the name of BMS-986278, and it is currently being evaluated in the phase 3 ALOFT program. One phase 3 study is targeting patients with IPF and has a primary completion date of October 26th, 2026. The second phase 3 study being done would be using BMS-986278 for the treatment of patients with Progressive Pulmonary Fibrosis [PPF]. This one has an estimated primary completion date of December 27th, 2027. This program for this big pharma is coming along well, as it received FDA Breakthrough Therapy Designation from the FDA for this drug back in October 2023. The point here is that with the MOA proven, plus the ability to possibly develop a best-in-class LPA1R agonist for IPF patients, I believe this is another solid long-term program. The truth is that this is not the only shot on goal in targeting these specific patients because ANPA-0073 as an APJR agonist inhibitor is also being developed for the treatment of patients with IPF. A phase 1 single-ascending and multiple-ascending dose study, testing this drug out, showed that the drug was well-tolerated with no serious adverse events [SAEs]. Again, it may also have been used as a muscle-sparing weight-loss drug.

Financials

According to the 10-Q SEC Filing, Structure Therapeutics had cash, cash equivalents and short-term investments of $927.1 million as of June 30th of 2024. The reason for the cash on hand is that the company was able to close an upsized underwritten public offering of 10,427,017 American Depositary Shares [ADSs], each representing three ordinary shares, including the full exercise of the underwriters' option to purchase up to 1,360,045 additional ADSs with a public offering price of $52.50 per ADS. The total aggregate gross proceeds from this offering were $547.4 million. The company is now well capitalized to reach through several key clinical catalysts. Why is that? That's because it believes that it has enough cash on hand to fund its operations through at least 2027.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Structure Therapeutics. The first risk to consider would be with respect to the development of GSBR-1290 for the treatment of patients with obesity. A phase 2b study is expected to begin in Q4 of 2024 and there is no assurance that the data to eventually be released from it will turn out to be positive. Nor, that the results to be released will be good enough to move forward toward phase 3 clinical testing.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of the possible combination therapies using the other drugs in its pipeline for the treatment of patients with obesity. As I noted directly above, there is an amylin inhibitor, GIPR inhibitor, and APJR inhibitor being developed alone at first. Should they pan out in initial phase 1 testing, then the goal would be to combine them with GLP-1 agonist GSBR-1290 to treat patients with obesity.

The third risk to consider would be in terms of competition in the obesity space. For instance, you have a lot of drugs either approved in clinical development to target the obesity market. One major competitor to beat would be Eli Lilly with Zepbound, which will not be an easy task. Especially, when you consider that the MOA of this drug is dual targeting of GLP-1 and GIP agonism. However, the downside with some drugs is that they are injectables. Thus, the company may compete well against this and many other drugs if it can achieve a competitive weight loss percentage, but at the same time also offer an oral route of administration instead. The risk here is that there is no assurance that GSBR-1290 will be shown to be superior to that of Zepbound or any other obesity drugs.

The fourth and final risk to consider would be in terms of developing an LPA1 receptor [LPA1R] agonist drug for the treatment of patients with IPF in the ongoing phase 1 study. The risk here is that there is no assurance that positive data will be achieved from this study for starters. Nor, that the results to be released will be enough to warrant further testing of this drug for the treatment of this patient population. In addition, while Bristol-Myers Squibb did establish the MOA of an LPA1 drug working in being able to treat these patients, there is no assurance that LTSE-2578 from Structure Therapeutics will do the same. Plus, you have to consider that Bristol-Myers Squibb's drug BMS-986278 might end up being a highly competitive drug if it is ultimately approved by regulatory authorities.

Conclusion

Structure Therapeutics has done well in developing its GLP-1R drug for the treatment of patients through the phase 2a study. As I have shown above, it has been able to achieve a substantial amount of weight loss with both GSBR-1290 given as a capsule or tablet for obese patients. This company is trying to make GLP-1 inhibition a more convenient option for these patients by giving them an oral alternative. Not only that, but the company hopes that it can get the drug out faster to patients. How so? Well, this would be through a more convenient manufacturing process. It believes that it can bring its process to scale to help >120 million patients. Plus, the fact that there is a huge safety window means that it can test out higher dosing in the next few studies. Again, it has several other drugs it is advancing individually to see if it can eventually combine them together with GSBR-1290 to achieve a greater weight-loss percentage for obese patients. It is also developing a few drugs to target patients with IPF as well, which are LTSE-2578 and ANPA-0073.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: VPU: Utilities In A Falling Rate Environment
data.description:
A utility stock is a share of a company providing essential public services, including water, electricity, or gas. Utilities are stable investments because the demand for crucial services tends to be recession-proof. Most utilities are government-regulated or have contractually guaranteed rates, making them less volatile than other stocks. Utilities are defensive investments because of their immunity to economic downturns. Moreover, utilities tend to pay attractive dividends as their revenues are stable.

The Vanguard Utilities Index Fund ETF Shares (NYSEARCA:VPU) owns shares of the leading public utilities, providing diversified exposure to the sector. Utility stocks can become attractive investment vehicles in a falling interest rate environment.

The Fed pivots to accommodative monetary policy

At the September meeting, the Fed’s FOMC chose an aggressive path to rate cuts. While the market had expected a 25 basis point reduction in the Fed Funds Rate, the committee decided that the inflation and jobs data warranted a 50 basis point cut. Moreover, the central bank told markets to expect another 50 basis points of short-term rate cuts by the end of 2024 and a further 100 basis point cut in 2025. If the Fed sticks to its current guidance, the Fed Funds Rate will stand at a midpoint of 3.375% by the end of next year, a full 2% below the 5.375% high in 2024.

Falling interest rates make dividend-paying stocks more attractive

The shift from a tight to a looser credit environment could support companies that pay investors substantial dividends. Stocks must compete with bonds for investment capital. While a falling interest rate environment tends to support equity prices, stocks paying steady dividends often thrive when rates decline. All companies are sensitive to the interest rate environment as it impacts financing costs that can support or impede growth. Utilities provide people with necessities, including electricity, gas, and water.

Meanwhile, addressing climate change has caused significant changes in the utility sector as companies shift to renewable energy sources that reduce carbon emissions. Utilities require substantial infrastructure and carry large debt loads on their balance sheets, making them sensitive to interest rate changes. Moreover, lower rates and a slowing economy tend to make utilities more attractive, as they provide investors with a steady dividend stream and stable share prices.

Utilities can be cash machines

Historically, utility stocks are stable investments that pay higher dividends than other equity sectors. The sector’s yield can make utility investments cash machines for conservative investors.

The average yield on the SPDR® S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY), which tracks the most diversified U.S. stock market index, the S&P 500 (SP500), is 1.20%. Investors allocate capital to the S&P 500 for capital growth, with dividends a small bonus. Meanwhile, the dividend yield on the Vanguard Utilities Index Fund ETF is at 2.83%, more than double the SPY.

VPU is the Vanguard Utilities Index Fund ETF product

The top holdings of the VPU ETF include:

At $175.14 per share, VPU had $8.46 billion in assets under management. VPU trades an average of over 200,000 shares daily and charges a 0.10% management fee. VPU’s fund profile states:

The utility ETF receives high ETF Grades from Seeking Alpha:

A grade in momentum, expenses, and liquidity and an A- in dividends make VPU a compelling ETF. The C in risk comes from tracking errors arising from the difference between VPU’s return and its benchmark, the MSCI U.S. Investible Market Index (“IMI”) 25/50.

A bullish trend with an above-market dividend yield

The trend is always an investor’s or trader’s best friend in any market. VPU’s trend has been bullish since 2004.

The chart of nominal share prices shows the VPU’s pattern of higher lows and higher highs since 2004. The VPU did well when short-term rates were at zero and when they rose to 5.375%.

The chart shows the rising share prices and increasing dividends over the past years. The SPY has outperformed The Utilities Select Sector SPDR® Fund ETF (XLU) since 2004.

With interest rates falling, U.S. political uncertainty, and the stock market near all-time highs, adding utilities to portfolios could be a conservative approach in the current environment. VPU is a product that offers stability and an above-market yield compared to the overall stock market.

data.title: Waste Management: Turning Trash To Cash
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman.

Waste Management, Inc. (NYSE:WM) is the largest waste management company in the U.S. and one of the largest in the world, without having significant exposure outside of North America. This is almost a utility-like business, as cash flows can be quite predictable, as trash removal is something nearly everyone needs this service. People and companies can do it themselves, but it often isn't convenient or even cost-effective; WM streamlines the process and can be competitive, given its significant size.

The company has an optimistic outlook for earnings and revenue to continue to grow, helped partially by its announced acquisition of Stericycle (SRCL) expected to close sometime this quarter. With strong earnings growth, this company can continue to keep up its dividend growth streak. Ultimately, this company turns trash (and recycling) into cash for its shareholders. We will learn more when the company reports Q3 earnings on Monday, October 28.

Earnings Review and Outlook

In the company's last earnings report for Q2 2024, they delivered a fairly rare miss on both the top and bottom lines. For both revenue and EPS estimates, that was only the fifth time in the last 16 quarters the company had missed expectations.

By far the largest business segment for the company is the collection and disposal, with recycling processing and sales a very distant but growing second. They also have a growing "WM Renewable Energy" segment.

The renewable energy business is through landfill gas-to-energy facilities; as trash decomposes, it releases methane gas that can be collected. Turning something that is literally waste into something useful to help power their natural gas fleet of vehicles. Further, it is expected to turn into additional tax credits as well.

The IRS issued proposed regulations applicable to the investment tax credits that could call into question our ability to realize some, or all, of this tax benefit, which would negatively impact financial expectations in connection with our significant planned and ongoing investments in sustainability growth projects in our WM Renewable Energy segment. In coordination with other members of the RNG industry and external advisors we are engaging directly with the U.S. Congress, the current federal administration, and other biogas sector stakeholders to encourage the Treasury Department to further refine its analysis prior to publication of final regulations that more accurately reflect the express language and legislative intent of the statute with respect to the investment tax credit. However, there is no guarantee that such efforts will be successful. We expect that the production tax credit incentives for investments in renewable energy and carbon capture, as expanded by the IRA, will likely result in an incremental benefit to the Company, although at this time, the anticipated amount of such benefit has not been quantified.

The North American waste management market is expected to be about $200 billion this year and grow around 4% annually. However, both earnings and revenue estimates show that WM is expected to grow even faster than that. In terms of revenue growth, it is a more modest ~6.3% average over the next five years.

On the other hand, EPS is expected to grow at nearly 11% annually over the coming five years. In particular, analysts are quite optimistic this year that WM can achieve some hefty growth. Then this moderates in the next two years but remains quite healthy at a just over double-digit pace before moderating a bit more after that. However, the further out we go, the fewer analysts who provide numbers, meaning the consensus can swing quite wildly.

While the company missed its expectations last quarter for earnings, there was a huge upside surprise in Q1 for the company. In the first half of the year, they've been able to deliver $3.57, leaving what would appear to be a realistic $3.70 they need to hit the estimates in the back half.

Helping to achieve that difference will be cost savings and also share buybacks. Along with the impressive dividend streak over the last 20 years, the company has been able to reduce its share count through repurchases. Over the last decade, that's been a trend outside of COVID-19 when they started to pare back a bit, and outstanding shares stayed about flat.

As we can see, though, even during Covid, the company's earnings did not take too much of a hit. This is thanks to its relatively stable business as an almost utility-like company. Then, the recovery post-Covid was swift, with 2021 being another strong year, pushing to even higher EPS than pre-Covid.

Further helping to drive potential growth for the company is the acquisition that is planned to happen this quarter of SRCL.

Stericycle has a leading position in the growing medical waste industry. The planned acquisition adds complementary business platforms to further our leading suite of comprehensive waste and environmental solutions. And these strategic benefits are accompanied by attractive financial benefits. Our team is progressing through the regulatory approval process and integration planning, and we're excited to welcome Stericycle's team members to WM.

They've highlighted that it is expected to be accretive to "WM's operating EBITDA and cash flows within one year of close." The acquisition is being funded through additional debt, and it'll push their leverage ratio to around 3.6x. They noted that they would expect to see their leverage ratio come back down to 2.75x to 3x in around 2 years. That's why share buybacks are expected to stop temporarily. Once they hit their leverage target, they'll resume repurchases, assuming all goes well.

In light of this elevated leverage, we're temporarily suspending our share repurchase program so that we can work our way back through our targeted leverage range of 2.75 times to 3 times, about 24 months after the close of Stericycle. The slight revisions in our projected leverage figures since our announcement of the planned acquisition of Stericycle are updates that reflect the impact of layering on the additional Tuck-In acquisition activity this year.

That's a fairly quick turnaround time to start seeing results, and if the acquisition closes as expected, we could start to see the benefits as soon as next year. However, the latest hold up is by Canadian regulators, as they've extended their review of the acquisition.

I think this is generally a favorable move; even though it will see share repurchases halted for a period and added leverage, it's focusing on growing the business. That can be considered a mostly more favorable move than share repurchases anyway; if they can do it successfully as a complementary business such as SRCL would seem to be an easy fit.

I find it particularly interesting that it will help to also provide a bit of a toe hold into new European markets. This is also providing some new exposure to customers in North America to help grow market share in their home markets as well.

Falling Into The Fair Value Range

The stock has been on quite a solid run this year, as the share price has lifted just over 16% through 2024. However, it was primarily the first quarter of this year and a significant pop early on that helped to lift the shares to where they are today. Since then, shares have been moving sideways. That said, that's not necessarily a negative, either.

That has helped to get the shares back into the fair value range based on its historical earnings multiple. Shares of WM are currently trading at a rather rich 28.6x forward P/E. With the S&P 500 Index (SP500) trading at what is considered a historically rich valuation and WM being even higher than that, it could be a concern for investors.

That said, on a relative basis to its historical range, that isn't all that rich at all. In fact, with the shares moving only sideways through most of this year and seeing earnings continue to grow, the shares have fallen back into their historical fair value range.

Investors with a long-term outlook of expecting to hold for years to come may not be so picky. However, more patient investors can try to wait until it falls below its fair value range, with a share price of ~$177. Alternatively, using a dollar-cost average approach is always an option for investors as well.

For what it is worth, analysts have an average price target of $222.50-suggesting that there is some upside to be had. Unfortunately, unlike the cold, hard data based on relative historical valuations, analysts tend to just adjust their price targets based on where shares are currently trading. If shares are rising, analysts will raise their price target and vice versa.

Getting Paid To Wait

One of the reasons to initiate a position now or through a DCA approach would be to start participating in receiving the cash this company spins out through its dividend every quarter. The company has delivered consistent dividend growth for 20 years now and at a fairly healthy pace. Over the last 5 years, the increases have averaged around 8%.

Given the expectation for continued earnings growth and a modest payout ratio of around 47.6% over the last year, that looks set to continue. Based on forward estimates, the payout ratio would come in at ~41.3%.

When looking at FCF per share, it comes out to a payout ratio of 57%-a bit more elevated, relatively speaking, but nothing to be overly concerned about either. Looking at it another way, they are expected FCF to hit $2 to $2.15 billion this year while expecting to pay just over $1.203 billion in dividends based on the current quarterly rate.

Conclusion

WM provides investors with a fairly steady business and has been able to deliver some pretty stellar earnings growth as well. With a planned acquisition, the company is set to grow further into a new complementary business. This will increase their leverage, which will put a pause on share repurchases. However, investing in the business for added growth should be a priority over buybacks anyway.

Waste Management, Inc. shares are looking richly priced on an absolute basis-but on a relative basis, that changes the picture to show that it's actually a better value now than it has been most of the last year. A more patient investor could wait for a more opportune entry price or utilize a dollar-cost average approach. An investor looking to hold for 3+ years probably doesn't need to be so picky anyway, assuming they can continue to grow earnings as expected.

data.description: The following slide deck was published by 3M Company in conjunction with their 2024 Q3 earnings call.
